author: 68000aa2d99d0f3b75749872561160bed7c2644f8b78d320
body: As Mozambique counts the cost of Cyclone Idai, the BBC's Africa editor Fergal Keane joined a helicopter flight over some of the worst-affected areas. The pilot pointed towards the east across the expanse of brown water. "We are 15 miles from the sea," he said, "but it feels as if we are right over it." He was right. Below us, farmland that had been home to thousands of people had disappeared. Charities say thousands of people are stranded by catastrophic flooding, clinging to roofs or stuck in trees. About 300 people are confirmed dead in Mozambique and Zimbabwe, but the toll is expected to rise. Please subscribe HERE http://bit.ly/1rbfUog

body: The port city of Beira in Mozambique was one of the worst affected places hit when Cyclone Idai swept over southeastern Africa. The storm killed more than one thousand people in Mozambique, Zimbabwe and Malawi. Hundreds of people are still missing as a result of the severe flooding that hit the region and outbreaks of cholera and malaria have added to the suffering. The World Bank estimates that three million people are still suffering the after-effects of the natural disaster. DW's Adrian Kriesch met with the mayor of Beira, who is fighting a daily battle to overcome the emergency left by the storm and to help his city move on. body: Five days after cyclone Idai tore through parts of southern Africa, the full extent of the devastation is slowly becoming clear. Homes and roads have been submerged or swept away and communities have been cut off. More than 1.5 million people have been affected. Agencies are desperately searching for the missing and they're getting satellite images help from NASA to help them. author: 9d7d26120bed5642daf037419210fbfdec7a844336007099
body: BEIRA, Mozambique — Hundreds of thousands of people are still without food, clean water or homes almost two weeks after Cyclone Idai tore through Mozambique, Zimbabwe and Malawi, killing at least 700 people and impacting millions more. Now, the first outbreak of cholera has been reported, sparking fears that this disaster recovery will soon become a long-term health emergency. The cyclone hit the port city of Beira at midnight on March 14, bringing winds of 125mph and torrential storms that burst the banks of two major rivers and flooded an area covering more than 1,600 square miles. A major international aid operation is now underway in Beira as waters begin to recede. But villagers in the hardest hit communities remain stranded while relief efforts struggle to reach them on roads decimated in the disaster. A lack of helicopters in the country has further hobbled response efforts. More than 100,000 people are now living in refugee camps. Many others are taking shelter in schools, churches and orphanages; their over-crowded temporary homes surrounded by pools of putrid flood water, which some are still using to drink and wash. Ignasio Augusto’s home, in the coastal village of Praia Nova, was decimated by the storm. “Nothing is left. The house fell down,” Ignasio said. He’s now sheltering with his family, packed alongside hundreds of others, in an elementary school which was itself partially destroyed by the winds. “This is how we sleep,” he said, pointing to the small plot of floor his family now occupies. “On sleeping bags, on the pavement. Women and men together,” Ignasio said. The cyclone’s destruction is breathtaking: Whole towns are flooded, roads are blocked by mud and bridges are ruined. The destruction stretches as far inland as Gôndola, more than 120 miles from the epicenter of the crisis. Even this far from the eye of the storm, the rains have made access slow or impossible. An aid team attempting to survey the damage using drones was stricken by mud during our journey. It took four hours of digging by men from a nearby town to free their 4x4 from the water. Only in the past few days has it become possible to pass — slowly — along the battered road to Beira Airport, which has become the makeshift headquarters of the international aid operation. Villagers here are facing the same problems as the hardest hit communities on the coast: food is not being delivered; supplies are not reaching them fast enough. Gerald Bourke, from the United Nations World Food Programme, told VICE News that despite the obstacles, the recovery is slowly coming together. “It’s gathering pace, but we need to do a lot more and we need to accelerate,” he said. But it could take several months to establish the true death toll and longer term impact of this storm on crops and food supplies. For now, the priority remains survival. “It has been difficult to get out there. You have communities who have been isolated for a long time. The full dimensions of it have yet to become clear.” Subscribe to VICE News here: http://bit.ly/Subscribe-to-VICE-News Check out VICE News for more: http://vicenews.com Follow VICE News here: Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/vicenews Twitter: https://twitter.com/vicenews Tumblr: http://vicenews.tumblr.com/ Instagram: http://instagram.com/vicenews More videos from the VICE network: https://www.fb.com/vicevideo

author: e8e0e76883ce2e92387dc4c2ff59ea96f4191f4776d650c7
body: (19 Mar 2019) More than 80 people were killed in Zimbabwe's eastern Chimanimani region after a cyclone slammed into the country, submerging entire villages and destroying nearly 1,000 homes. One local resident told an AP reporter that 26 people from the area were buried on Monday. Tinashe Sithole said he walked for 28 kilometres (17 miles) as his village has been cut off by gushing floodwaters in the aftermath of the deadly cyclone. UN agencies and the Red Cross helped rush emergency food and medicine by helicopter to the stricken countries. Meanwhile more than 1,000 people were feared dead in Mozambique, where Cyclone Idai first struck, with 84 confirmed dead so far. According to official figures, more than 50 people were killed in Malawi, bringing the total number of casualties in the three countries to over 215. The death toll, however, is set to rise as hundreds more are reported missing. body: Seven days after Cyclone Idai made landfall in Africa, a lot of people are still struggling to cope with the aftermath. Mozambique has declared a national state of emergency as it faces catastrophic flooding in Idai's wake. Idai turned lowlands near the city of Beira into an inland sea. More rain is expected in the coming days and the dire situation for tens of thousands of survivors is expected to get worse. body: Cyclone Idai is on track to becoming Southern Africa's worst natural disaster. That's according to the United Nations. At least 360 people have died and thousands more displaced in Mozambique, Zimbabwe and Malawi. The death toll in Zimbabwe alone has increased to 139. And the number is likely to rise even further. Chimanimani is one the areas that took a severe beating. Our Zimbabwe correspondent, Pindai Dube joins us live now from Mutare. Courtesy #DStv403

author: a14d326311c0dd42593d2980bbd13a3d68f925aec838503c
body: The death toll has risen to at least 215 after Cyclone Idai tore into central Mozambique last week before continuing on to Zimbabwe and Malawi, bringing flash floods and ferocious winds. At least 126 people were killed in Mozambique and Malawi, according to the Red Cross, and Zimbabwe's information ministry on Monday put the number of dead at 89 in the country. Most of the deaths in Mozambique happened in the central port city of Beira, 90 percent of which was destroyed, according to the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC). A large dam burst on Sunday in the city, cutting off the last road to the city of about 530,000 people, the IFRC said in a statement. Euloge Ishimwe, the Africa region communications manager for the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, talks to Al Jazeera about the disastrous cyclone. - Subscribe to our channel: http://aje.io/AJSubscribe - Follow us on Twitter: https://twitter.com/AJEnglish - Find us on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/aljazeera - Check our website: https://www.aljazeera.com/ #AlJazeeraEnglish #CycloneIdai #Mozambique

body: A year after eastern Zimbabwe was hit by Cyclone Idai, one of the worst tropical storms for Africa on record, some survivors are still recovering. The cyclone killed hundreds of people in Zimbabwe and left thousands homeless. Authorities have been rebuilding but many are still living in temporary housing, as Columbus Mavhunga reports from Chimanimani District, on the border with Mozambique. author: 3e37cd830b250bf61e0f89f53e24ca3ed174fa98950f8c68
body: In March 2019, southern Africa was hit by a tropical cyclone, rated amongst the worst ever experienced on the continent. Cyclone Idai left a trail of devastation and killed at least 1,300 people in Zimbabwe, Mozambique and Malawi. In Zimbabwe, the storm affected more than 270 000 people leaving 341 dead and many others missing. 17 608 households were left homeless. Survivors in Zimbabwe are still living in tents and temporary shelters provided by humanitarian agencies and are desperate to move out into permanent structures they have been promised by the government. Road infrastructure was grossly damaged with above 90% of road networks in Chimanimani and Chipinge destroyed. Landslides destroyed 584 km of roads. Almost all bridges in Chimanimani District were swept away rendering the area inaccessible. However, a lot of effort has been made to repair the bridges and roads that are now in a good condition. In terms of relocating people, 220 internally displaced households are going to be resettled at West End farm an area close to Nhedziwa Business Centre, some 50 kilometres from Chimanimani. 55 housing units from the targeted 220 have been constructed and Government is targeting to have completed 105 housing units by the end of June 2021.

author: a8f7b9ab6d061471275a1aea5e7230f566f2aac3408304e3
body: Cyclone Idai didn’t just kill 1,500 people; decades of "manufactured vulnerability" did. While the storm was a natural force, the catastrophic loss of life was driven by institutional failure, poverty, and corruption. In March 2019, Idai slammed into Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi—nations already weakened by drought. This video explores the "social autopsy" of the disaster, revealing how emergency warnings were issued in languages many victims didn't speak and how early warning systems failed entirely. We also uncover the harrowing human rights abuses that followed: • Language Barriers: Warnings in Portuguese ignored rural communities speaking Sena and Ndau. • Aid Corruption: Relief supplies were distributed along partisan lines in Zimbabwe. • Predatory Behavior: Women were coerced into exchanging sex for food by local leaders. Years later, with 240,000 homes damaged and recovery stalled, we ask: who does the system really protect?. This video is a shared journey towards greater awareness, crafted with AI assistance and validated with care. However, the quest for knowledge is never complete. I welcome your insights and corrections in the comments below, as they enrich our collective understanding. Together, let's explore and learn. Thank you for joining this exploration. Learn more about the investigation: • Human Rights Watch: hrw.org • Cyclone Idai Recovery Reports: worldbank.org #cyclone #idai #climatejustice #humanrights #mozambique #naturaldisaster #globalissues

body: In March 2019 Cyclone Idai, a category 4 storm, hit Mozambique. It smashed into the coastal city of Beira and led to an unexpected and devastating chain of events. Flooding on a biblical scale overwhelmed the government and local aid workers. A Perfect Storm charts this extraordinary story as the world raced to help rescue and support survivors and bring order to the chaos. body: It's been more than three weeks now since the devastating Cyclone Idai made landfall. While the waters might have receded the true extent of the damage is only now being assessed. author: 523a83c54bc7c2f4778670023880e6f95a6d009f0ce54a85
body: CURATED BY RAPHAEL CHIKUKWA AND JEKESAI NJIKIZANA Cyclone Idai made landfall in Zimbabwe across Chipinge, Chimanimani, Bikita and Buhera in March of 2019. The most affected areas where Chimanimani and Chipinge. It was said to be one of the worst Indian Ocean tropical cyclones to affect the Southern Hemisphere. The storm caused catastrophic damage in Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi, leaving more than 1,000 people dead and thousands more missing. The disaster resulted in the loss of lives destruction of crops and livestock; destruction of infrastructure, collapsed lines of communication and road networks, the displacement of internal populations and the fragmentation of families. The cyclone’s exact death toll is not known. The challenges and risks posed by such natural disasters constitute a trans threat to human and wildlife security in the region. Climate change has meant that the severity and frequency of storms has increased and is likely to be on the rise. Drought, warmer air temperatures and rising sea levels mean storm surges will be more powerful, as seen with Cyclone Idai. To mark a year since Cyclone Idai, the National Gallery of Zimbabwe and its partners and sponsors brings to you this exhibition as a reflective project to educate and provoke dialogue on issues pertaining climate change and global warming, disaster preparedness, displacement and many others issues brought about by natural disasters. Furthermore After Shock: Re Imagining Life after Cyclone Idai exhibition also seeks to educate those who only heard about the cyclone without seeing the magnitude of its impact. The post-disaster context provides necessary insights into art practice as the socio-cultural work of processing the human experience of disaster and aiding in empathic understanding of trauma. Artistic and Cultural responses, such as this exhibition and its public programming are significant in processing and analysing the human experience of disaster, and they also establish a basis for empathising with those affected. View the Exhibition at; http://www.nationalgallery.co.zw/index.php/see/whats-on/running-exhibition/169-after-shock-re-imagining-life-after-cyclone-idai

author: ea1cfcbb0ec668225f1a43589c53214e1924e5b16252134e
body: Zimbabwe experienced Tropical Cyclone Idai from the 15th of March 2019 to the 17th of March 2019 which saw an average of 600 mm of rainfall per day been recorded and caused massive destruction in Manicaland and Masvingo provinces of Zimbabwe. The hardest-hit district is Chimanimani which is in Manicaland province and has remained largely inaccessible due to damaged roads and main access bridges. Landslides also blocked some road sections cutting the district out from the rest of the province. Telecom networks and power lines were destroyed in Chimanimani and Chipinge cutting off communication channels and causing power outages. In Chimanimani, the water supply network, treatment and pumping stations, sewage system and sewer treatment plants were extensively damaged. Productive assets such as dip-tanks were flooded and shopping centres washed away. Oxfam in Zimbabwe responded to the disaster aimed at: Oxfam’s objectives of the Response were: • To promote personal, menstrual and household hygiene through the provision of NFIs, clean water, appropriate sanitation and hygiene information to the affected households. • To prevent or reduce the likely outbreak of water and vector borne diseases through hygiene promotion and NFIs distribution. • To provide appropriate sanitation facilities through the construction of emergency latrines and temporary bath shelters in the affected areas during the emergency phase and construction of institutional sanitation facilities for schools and hospitals in the recovery stage. • To provide direct food aid in the form of food hampers to the affected households to save people from starvation. • To restore livelihoods of the affected areas through training in entrepreneurship and providing ISAL start up kits for proposed income generation projects in the post emergency phase. • To disseminate critical WASH and EFSVL information. The response’s overall goal is to reduce morbidity and mortality due to Cholera amongst communities in Manicaland Province.

In Chimanimani, the water supply network, treatment and pumping stations, sewage system and sewer treatment plants were extensively damaged. Productive assets such as dip-tanks were flooded and shopping centres washed away. Oxfam in Zimbabwe responded to the disaster aimed at: Oxfam’s objectives of the Response were: • To promote personal, menstrual and household hygiene through the provision of NFIs, clean water, appropriate sanitation and hygiene information to the affected households. • To prevent or reduce the likely outbreak of water and vector borne diseases through hygiene promotion and NFIs distribution. • To provide appropriate sanitation facilities through the construction of emergency latrines and temporary bath shelters in the affected areas during the emergency phase and construction of institutional sanitation facilities for schools and hospitals in the recovery stage. • To provide direct food aid in the form of food hampers to the affected households to save people from starvation. • To restore livelihoods of the affected areas through training in entrepreneurship and providing ISAL start up kits for proposed income generation projects in the post emergency phase. • To disseminate critical WASH and EFSVL information. The response’s overall goal is to reduce morbidity and mortality due to Cholera amongst communities in Manicaland Province.

body: At least 31 people died in eastern Zimbabwe after tropical cyclone Idai battered the country. Dozens are missing as bridges and homes were swept away by the storm. Cyclone Idai, which brought floodwater and destruction to areas of Mozambique and Malawi, hit Zimbabwe on Friday, cutting off power and communications. The information ministry said the army had moved in to rescue 197 pupils trapped at a local school.

body: Cyclone Idai remains a Category 3 storm this morning, despite a collapse of its eye structure overnight due to upwelling and dry air intrusion. The storm is still uncomfortably close to the western coast of Madagascar, and tropical storm force winds are still likely along the coast. In Mozambique, Idai is expected to arrive on Thursday or Friday, and could deliver a further 500mm of rainfall as it stalls and turns back out over the Channel. 40 are known to have been killed in Mozambique and Malawi from the precursor system to Idai. body: Aid workers are desperately trying to get emergency supplies to tens of thousands of people hit by cyclone Idai that swept across Southern Africa. An estimated 1.7 million people have been affected. In Mozambique, which was worst hit, 293 have now been confirmed dead. Delivering relief to many of the survivors remains a struggle. Damaged bridges and blocked roads have delayed access to many areas. Al Jazeera's Tony Birtley was with a community in Tika in Mozambique as aid arrived one week after the storm hit. - Subscribe to our channel: http://aje.io/AJSubscribe - Follow us on Twitter: https://twitter.com/AJEnglish - Find us on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/aljazeera - Check our website: https://www.aljazeera.com/ #AlJazeeraEnglish #CycloneIdai #Mozambique

author: 255c6759bfba0d1a54c9ebdd2611ea1b1cfaf397ed78d48f
body: Children in Mozambique who have been made orphans by Cyclone Idai are slowly being evacuated from flood hit areas - in rescues reminiscent of those in Dunkirk during WWII. They have been rescued and ferried to safety but are completely on their own - their families did not survive. The rescue operation out of Beira, Mozambique's fourth largest city and one of the most badly affected by the cyclone and flooding - 90% of all buildings were damaged or destroyed - has involved local fishermen volunteering to help. Without the assistance of aid, fishermen with boats of varying sizes headed to cut off areas and collected as many people as possible - just like British sailors did following the Battle of Dunkirk. • Subscribe to ITV News on YouTube: http://bit.ly/2lOHmNj • Get breaking news and more stories at http://www.itv.com/news Follow ITV News on Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/itvnews/ Follow ITV News on Twitter: https://twitter.com/itvnews Follow ITV News on Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/itvnews/

author: 9b1a6aa2f1f4b3a4682ad6c47ed4921a73cc229f0a1a1dd8
body: More than 100 people are dead after a devastating cyclone barrelled through our neighbours, Mozambique and Zimbabwe. Cyclone Idai has now largely dissipated. But earlier it bought floods and ferocious winds after making landfall near the port city of Beira in Mozambique. Zimbabwe says that 65 people were killed in the east of the country. While the UN in Zimbabwe says nearly 10 000 were affected. Mozambique has raised its own death toll to 48. Many are still missing after houses and bridges were washed away and some areas are still not accessible. Even before the cyclone made landfall on Friday heavy rains had claimed more than 100 lives in Mozambique and Malawi. President Cyril Ramaphosa has confirmed that South Africans have been sent to help electricity authorities in Mozambique to try to restore power. The cut off of extra energy has contributed to the current load-shedding that South Africa is experiencing. International Relations Minister, Lindiwe Sisulu has appealed to NGOs, companies and individuals to make donations for the flood victims. To discuss we're joined by the spokesperson for the Department of International Relations and Cooperation, Ndivhuwo Mabaya. For more news, visit: sabcnews.com

author: 8f3c40b1316fb6655597aa0ce7b6aeab04e408115745c370
body: This video highlights the impact of the South-to-South Cooperation Assistance Fund (SSCAF) project titled “Health Assistance for Women and Girls Affected by Tropical Cyclone Idai in Zimbabwe,” jointly implemented by UNFPA Zimbabwe and the Embassy of the People’s Republic of China. Focused on Manicaland Province, particularly Chimanimani and Chipinge districts—the areas hardest hit by Cyclone Idai—the project aimed to restore access to essential maternal health services disrupted by the cyclone in 2019 and further strained by the COVID-19 pandemic. - Through this partnership, the project promoted: - Safe deliveries and emergency obstetric care - Rehabilitation of maternal health infrastructure - Provision of medical supplies and training for healthcare workers - Community outreach to ensure no woman or girl is left behind This video showcases the stories, progress, and hope made possible through strong global collaboration and a shared commitment to women's health and rights.

author: feb5c2391216514caafd4fa750fef275c6ffb2316f13259a
body: Video message by António Guterres, Secretary-General of the United Nations, on the 2nd anniversary of Cyclone Idai. As we mark the second anniversary of Tropical Cyclone Idai, the United Nations is honoured to continue standing in solidarity with the people and Government of Mozambique. In the aftermath of the unprecedented back-to-back Cyclone Idai and Cyclone Kenneth, I travelled to the country and saw the devastation and recovery efforts firsthand. I will never forget it. I was deeply moved by the strength and resilience of all those affected — and I was also inspired by the heroism of first responders. The force of the storm is a reminder that time is running out for the world to act on climate change. Tropical storms are becoming more intense and more frequent.  Parts of Africa are warming at twice the global rate. Indeed, Africa is the least responsible for climate disruption yet is among the first and worst to suffer.  The world must take immediate action to mitigate global warming while supporting nations on the frontline of climate change to build resilience and adapt to impacts. Two years after Cyclone Idai, so many families still struggle to rebuild their lives. Tropical Storm Chalane hit in December 2020 followed by Tropical Cyclone Eloise in January 2021. These storms were emergencies on top of emergencies. The people of Mozambique urgently need our help to tackle the triple threat of conflict, the climate crisis, and the COVID-19 pandemic. I call on the international community to step up and support the humanitarian response plan for Mozambique, which needs $254 million to respond to escalating humanitarian needs brought on by the triple crises. On this solemn anniversary, let’s join hands to help the people of Mozambique recover better together.

author: 21d71fea6850d4be13da221a5832066c7ab9fb457e3ddad9
body: 19 March 2019, #Beira, #Mozambique, Red Cross teams receive emergency shelter supplies in Beira, Mozambique, and prepare to distribute aid to #CycloneIdai survivors. With reports that at least 400,000 people have been made homeless in central Mozambique, the world’s largest humanitarian network is warning that the full extent of the “humanitarian catastrophe” caused by Tropical Cyclone Idai may take days to become clear. Large areas to the west of Beira have been severely flooded. In some areas close to the Buzi and Pungwe rivers, flood water are metres deep and have completely covered homes, telephone poles and trees. IFRC and Mozambique Red Cross have launched a 10 million Swiss franc emergency appeal to support about 75,000 of the worst affected people in central Mozambique. The appeal prioritizes shelter, and water and sanitation.

body: More than 100 people are dead after a devastating cyclone barrelled through our neighbours, Mozambique and Zimbabwe. Cyclone Idai has now largely disappated. But earlier it bought floods and ferocious winds after making landfall near the port city of Beira in Mozambique. Zimbabwe says that 65 people were killed in the east of the country. While the UN in Zimbabwe says nearly 10 000 were affected. Mozambique has raised its own death toll to 48. Many are still missing after houses and bridges were washed away and some areas are still not accessible. Even before the cyclone made landfall on Friday heavy rains had claimed more than 100 lives in Mozambique and Malawi. President Cyril Ramaphosa has confirmed that South Africans have been sent to help electricity authorities in Mozambique to try to restore power. The cut off of extra energy has contributed to the current load-shedding that South Africa is experiencing. Involved in relief efforts here are Zanu-PF spokesperson in South Africa, Kennedy Mandaza and Chenjerai Kangausaru who is also a Zanu PF politician. For more news, visit: sabcnews.com

body: In March 2019, Mozambique, Malawi and Zimbabwe were hit by the heaviest natural disaster in the region, Cyclone Idai. In Zimbabwe the Chipinge and Chimanimani areas where the hardest hit. More than 750 people lost their lives and tens of thousands were left homeless. Family AIDS Caring Trust Zimbabwe (FACT)with the support of funding from the Tear partners (Tear Netherlands and Tear-fund UK) was one of the first responders and provided support to the Chimanimani and Chipinge communities. The Relief Aid by FACT facilitated the establishment of Self-Help Groups (SHGs) to allow groups of young women to save money collectively, trained lead farmers on Conservation Agriculture focusing on Maize and beans production, provided Psycho-Social Support to the victims and targeted Vulnerable Communities including child headed families by assisting beneficiaries with food hampers, farming tools such as shovels, hoes, slashers; and cane knives. "By Love Serve One Another" #ReliefAID #EmpoweringCommunities

body: It has been a month since Cyclone Idai made landfall in Mozambique, but government and aid agencies are racing against time to prevent a looming famine from becoming worse. More than 700,000 hectares of crop fields were destroyed by the raging floodwaters following the cyclone. CGTN's Frances Kuo spoke with Russell Geekie for more on the aftermath. Geekie is the Chief of Media Relations and Spokesperson for the United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs.

author: e8e0e76883ce2e92387dc4c2ff59ea96f4191f4776d650c7
body: (24 Mar 2019) Local fishermen continued to ferry displaced people from Buzi to the port city of Beira on Saturday, as a second week of efforts got underway to find and help tens of thousands of people after Cyclone Idai devastated a large swath of Mozambique. On Saturday, children and barefoot women were helped off a fishing boat by aid workers on the rainy shores of Beira. One of them was Christina Machado, who arrived with her two children and a bandage on her ankle, which she said was cut by a tin roof during the cyclone and just treated on Friday.   "I'm looking for my husband," she said. He had been working in Beira for two months. She didn't know where she would be taken next. Travelling against the ongoing traffic of displaced people moving towards the shattered city of Beira, now home to crowded camps, Veronica Fatia huddled on a wooden boat on her journey back to Buzi in the hope of finding her mother alive. Carrying bags of rice and her two-year-old daughter, Fatia stepped carefully off the boat and walked into the remains of Buzi. More than a week after Cyclone Idai roared in, the muddy floodwaters were still pouring off the river banks, draining what aid workers had called an inland ocean. She found her mother, Maria Antonio, at a school - the happiness of the reunion tempered with fears for another daughter who had been going to school in Quelimane and has not been heard from since the cyclone hit. Communications have been badly affected by the cyclone, separating families in the chaos. "I don't know how she is surviving, if she is even alive," Antonio said. Throughout the region, emergency air efforts are focuing on spotting stranded people and communities in the hope of dropping aid or plucking those in dire need to safety. There are signs of life in the inundated landscape, even smoke from some cooking fires. As waters recede, however, aid workers expect the death toll to rise as bodies are found. With water and sanitation systems largely destroyed, waterborne diseases are also a growing concern. Carrying bags of rice and her two-year-old daughter, Fatia stepped carefully off the boat and walked into the remains of Buzi. More than a week after Cyclone Idai roared in, the muddy floodwaters were still pouring off the river banks, draining what aid workers had called an inland ocean. She found her mother, Maria Antonio, at a school - the happiness of the reunion tempered with fears for another daughter who had been going to school in Quelimane and has not been heard from since the cyclone hit. Communications have been badly affected by the cyclone, separating families in the chaos. "I don't know how she is surviving, if she is even alive," Antonio said. Throughout the region, emergency air efforts are focuing on spotting stranded people and communities in the hope of dropping aid or plucking those in dire need to safety. There are signs of life in the inundated landscape, even smoke from some cooking fires. As waters recede, however, aid workers expect the death toll to rise as bodies are found. With water and sanitation systems largely destroyed, waterborne diseases are also a growing concern. body: Cyclone Idai hit Mozambique and the borders of Zimbabwe on 15 March 2019 with winds up to 177km/h. The death toll has risen to over 700 with many more missing. Flooding caused by the tropical cyclone has caused massive destruction to buildings, crops and livestock. It has been called by the U.N as potentially “one of the worst climate-related disasters to hit Southern Africa” As Hillsong Church South Africa, through our Hillsong Africa Foundation, we will be collecting donations to assist with the relief efforts done in the area. Donations will aid in the medical and rescue assistance to victims where needed. Our hearts are with our African family. Together we can help bring support to those devastated by this disaster.

body: MSF is running mobile clinics in areas of Beira, a city hit hard by Cyclone Idai. During these clinics our staff provide care for patients suffering for infected cuts and small wounds, respiratory tract infections, skin conditions as well as diarrhoea. Importantly, they also go door to door looking for cases of diarrhoea that may need treatment at the local health centres. Diarrhoea can be easily treated with oral or IV rehydration as well as antibiotics. During the mobile clinic, if the team finds a patient in need of inpatient care they are transferred to the health centre or hospital by MSF. Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) is an international, independent, medical humanitarian organisation that delivers emergency aid to people affected by armed conflict, epidemics, natural disasters and exclusion from healthcare. body: (28 May 2019) LEAD IN: Cyclone Idai struck southern Africa in March, the third deadliest cyclone in the southern hemisphere on record. Two months on survivors start to piece their lives back together. STORYLINE: One of the many villages devastated by Cyclone Idai. Survivor Ellen Madson's home is now rubble. A few remnants of the past are found, like this mud-covered teapot. Cyclone Idai swept through Mwalija village and the Malawi region in March. Madson recalls the events, "the water levels increased quickly and we had to seek a high point." The water levels kept increasing she says, "one of my children climbed that tree to reach safety. Another man also climbed another tree behind here. But even the trees were becoming submerged. We managed to find a high concrete structure and when we got there we gave up. We didn't think any of us would survive. It was by the grace of God that we made it. We were rescued." Madson and other villagers spent 21 hours on an elevated concrete structure, until being rescued by a boat sent by the government. Before the cyclone hit Madson had a field of crops, food supplies and livestock. She lost everything in the flooding. Like other survivors from Mwalija village, Madson has had to build a shelter at the Mwalija internally displaced people's camp. Located just 1.5 kilometres away from her village, this shelter is on higher ground. There are 365 households living here. This is one of the 173 internally displaced people's camps in Malawi, according to the UNHCR. Rainford Kwala, Chairman of the Mwalija Camp explains, "there are many challenges here. Even the structures that you see here are not suitable for a family. Parents and children living in the same small space." UNHCR Malawi Country Representative, Monique Ekoko explains, "the effects in Malawi were quite devastating." The landlocked country in south-central Africa ranks among some of the world's least developed countries. "More than 800,000 people were affected. We have about 86,000 of them displaced. 60 deaths. About 600 and something injuries. And for Malawi, this is big, given the serious socio-economic challenges that the country faces on its own." The Malawi Red Cross Society stepped in by creating new homes for displaced people. "Many homes have been destroyed. A number of communities are relocating from lower land to higher land. As Malawi Red Cross we are supporting with transitional shelters. In Chikwawa we are supporting with 100 transitional shelters for 100 households. This type of shelter is going to help the flood survivors to have a starting point in terms of housing. For now, we have started to construct for the community of Mwalija where we have already built the five pilot shelters, and are continuing with the 95 further shelters," explains Cecilia Banda, Malawi Red Cross Society Chikwawa District Coordinator. There are over 100 households in this camp, identified as the most vulnerable and in need of new homes. Madson was able to secure a shelter, she explains: "The house means a lot to me. Of course, it's not permanent, or solid. I will need something more permanent. Something built with bricks. I lost almost everything, so it will take some time to rebuild my life. But I don't wish to return to my old village. Here we are looking to the future." Harris Kachale is Director of Disaster Response and Recovery and says the government is working hard to identify more higher-lying land for resettlement. The Red Cross temporary homes settlement is not perfect, lacking, for example, a water source at this stage. body: #CycloneIdai continues to proceed towards the city of Beira, Mozambique, as a Category 3 storm, where winds of 100kph are currently occurring with worse on the way. The storm's winds are likely to be severe, but its surge and flooding will be the main cause of devastation from this storm over the next five days across Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi, who have already suffered at least 71 fatalities and 577 injuries so far. Landfall is expected after dark this evening, with rapid dissipation likely afterwards. The rains, however, will long outlive its winds. body: Tropical cyclone Idai, the biggest to hit Mozambique in over a decade, made landfall on Thursday evening just north of the port city of Beira injuring at least five people. The strongest winds around the eye were about 160km an hour, blowing from the south across the city. Its 500,000 residents are now without electricity and communications have been severed, the National Institute of Disaster Management said.

body: The resilience of infrastructure systems to climate shocks has a huge bearing on the survival of communities in the event of disaster. As the effects of climate change continue to manifest, it is vital that policy makers and planners adopt evidence based approaches thereby ensuring improvements to the climate proofing and resilience of infrastructure systems. Join this conversation on the role of infrastructure in disaster risk reduction and mapping a resilient future for Zimbabwe.

body: After Cyclone Idai's devastating impact in March 2019, the community faced immense challenges: damaged roads, landslide risks, destroyed water sources, and loss of lives and livestock. Yet, in the face of adversity, the village came together to rebuild and renew hope. Dalend Chikukwa gives firsthand account of how individuals like Silas Chemura, John Njanji and others, together with community organisations like CELUO and CRT restored their land, revived their rivers, and secured a brighter future for generations to come.

body: When Cyclone Idai hit Mozambique in 2019, humanitarian teams faced one of the worst disasters in recent history. Entire communities were submerged, roads vanished, and access collapsed. In those critical hours, GIS (Geographic Information System) became a lifeline, helping responders map floodwaters, locate isolated villages, and plan safe routes for rescue. This video tells the real story of how GIS Technology and trained humanitarian professionals worked together to turn uncertainty into coordinated, life-saving action. What this video covers: – How GIS application in crisis supported emergency response during Cyclone Idai – The role of satellite imagery and drone mapping in crisis conditions – Why GIS matters in humanitarian work, access and decision-making – How map data guided rescue teams when every second mattered – Why GIS training is now essential for NGOs and humanitarian professionals Who this video is for: – Humanitarian workers – NGO and INGO professionals – Disaster response teams – GIS analysts and mapping specialists – Emergency planners & coordinators – Students and professionals entering the humanitarian field Why this matters: In modern humanitarian operations, GIS is not optional. It is the backbone of crisis mapping, access negotiation, logistics planning, and real-time decision support. But tools alone aren’t enough—trained professionals turn maps into action. At Indepth Research (IRES), we equip humanitarian and NGO professionals with GIS skills for emergencies, helping them navigate complex crises ethically, strategically, and confidently. Explore GIS training for humanitarians: https://www.indepthresearch.org/course/gis-for-humanitarian-assistance-training-course

body: Cyclone Idai made landfall in Mozambique on the 14th of March 2019, leaving behind a devastating humanitarian crisis. Over 600 people died and more than 100 000 homes were destroyed by the cyclone. The events of cyclone Idai have been described as the worst natural disaster to ever hit the African continent. As a result of this catastrophe, the Midnight Train to Beira was formed by South African storytelling group, Hadithi ya Africa, aimed to collect and deliver aid to the hard-hit Beira which they successfully did, now they have launched the Midnight train phase two which will be focussing on building houses for the victims. Thandwefika Tshabalala is the founder of Hadithi ya Africa and he joins now us in the studio. For more news, visit: sabcnews.com

body: This video aims to raise awareness on Protecting and Promoting the Rights and Dignity of Persons with Disabilities in Cyclone Idai Affected Areas in Zimbabwe. Its main objective include highlighting the dire challenges faced by and needs of persons with disabilities affected by Cyclone Idai in Chimanimani, Chipinge and Bikita. Expected outcomes include, increased awareness among partners and development actors on the needs of persons with disabilities in in the mentioned areas above and strengthened stakeholder networks mobilized towards building a disability inclusive early warning systems and post–disaster recovery and rebuilding.

Its main objective include highlighting the dire challenges faced by and needs of persons with disabilities affected by Cyclone Idai in Chimanimani, Chipinge and Bikita. Expected outcomes include, increased awareness among partners and development actors on the needs of persons with disabilities in in the mentioned areas above and strengthened stakeholder networks mobilized towards building a disability inclusive early warning systems and post–disaster recovery and rebuilding.

body: Humanitarian group - Gift of the Givers has sent rescue teams to assist with relief efforts in Malawi and Mozambique hit by Tropical Cyclone Idai. Mozambique's President Filipe Nyusi said yesterday that more than one-thousand people are feared dead. Nyusi said that although the official death count currently stands at 84, he believes the toll could be be more than one-thousand. Close to 1-million people have also been displaced and thousands of homes destroyed. Gift of the Givers Founder Imtiaz Sooliman joins us now from our studio in Durban. author: 4c97c3ad7260aa57f7509bf32f9b3698e608c106a4aee843
body: On Friday 14 March, Cyclone Idai made landfall along the south-eastern coast of Africa. With sustained wind speeds of 120mph and heavy rain, it is now recognised as one of the most intense recorded weather events to hit the region. Many affected areas were already heavily waterlogged, resulting in extensive flooding. MapAction volunteer Andreas Buchholz returned from Beira, Mozambique, at the beginning of April. He put together this short video which gives a good sense of the scale and urgency of the international response to this major disaster. MapAction has been working closely with the government of Mozambique, NGOs, the UN and Red Cross teams at the heart of the response and our help has been widely appreciated. The aerial assessment maps shown in the video have so far been printed over two thousand times and used to support search and rescue and the distribution of foodstuffs. The maps were created by the MapAction team in Mozambique with close collaboration with the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (UNOCHA) and Save the Children. At the time of writing, MapAction personnel are continuing to work at the On-Site Operations Coordination Centre in Beira, Mozambique.

body: The ocean does not always stay where it belongs. In March 2019, a powerful storm rose from the warm waters of the Indian Ocean and became one of the deadliest natural disasters in African history. Cyclone Idai did not just bring wind — it brought water, destruction, and a humanitarian crisis that reshaped entire nations. This cinematic documentary explores the full story of Cyclone Idai — from its birth over unusually warm ocean waters to the devastating floods that swept across Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi. Discover: How tropical cyclones form and gain strength from ocean heat Why Cyclone Idai became so powerful and destructive The role of coastal geology and low-lying deltas in amplifying flood impact How storm surge and river flooding combined into a catastrophic inland sea The deadly landslides in mountainous regions like Chimanimani The humanitarian crisis that followed — displacement, disease, and loss What this disaster reveals about climate change and future extreme weather Entire cities were submerged. Rivers became oceans. Mountains turned into landslides.

author: 604140b7e2645bd4cc3eb3c4e7d9ad707ac16808e6c00f83
body: In March 2019, Cyclone Idai struck southeastern Africa with catastrophic force, devastating parts of Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi. Entire cities were submerged, roads and bridges washed away, and more than 1,300 people lost their lives. The storm triggered massive flooding, landslides, and a humanitarian crisis that left millions in urgent need of food, shelter, and medical care. In this video, we explore the terrifying path of Cyclone Idai, the scale of destruction, and the resilience of communities fighting to rebuild after one of the worst tropical cyclones in Southern Hemisphere history.

author: cc40f0ddf895f5fd3cf330546101b244e63fea150b4a06b2
body: In March 2019, cyclone Idai slammed into Mozambique, Malawi and Zimbabwe with speeds of more than 200 kilometres per hour, causing severe damage. One month after Idai, a second storm hit northern Mozambique while the country was still recovering from cyclone Idai. More than 600 people died and 1,600 were injured during the unprecedented disasters of Cyclones Idai and Kenneth. Furthermore, hundreds of thousands of people have been displaced, crops have been destroyed and livelihoods lost as a result of these storms. It is the first time in recorded history that two strong tropical cyclones have hit the country during the same season. An estimated 750,000 people still require immediate assistance. The impact of the cyclones goes well beyond food insecurity, negatively affecting nutrition, health, education, water and sanitation. Give a monthly gift to help refugees here: https://donate.careinternational.org.uk/page/51917/donate/1 Cyclone Idai destroyed more than 700,000 hectares of crops including maize, ground nut, cassava, beans and rice. All of the fields have been indurated. Experts estimate that the agricultural loss in Mozambique is at least USD 141 million high. More than 73,000 people are living in temporary shelters, most of them are schools and community buildings. Over 3,000 classrooms have been destroyed, over 330,000 students have been affected. What is CARE doing? CARE has assisted more than 300,000 people affected by the crisis with food assistance, access to water and education, shelter and drought-resistant seeds. This also includes the provision of hygiene items including emergency household items such as tarpaulins, blankets, mosquito nets, sleeping mats, bathing soap, and jerry cans. In the aftermath of the Cyclone, CARE built 360 temporary learning spaces, ensuring lessons continue for some 28.800 students. Additionally, CARE is repairing two severely damaged health centres and 130 classrooms. New classrooms and health centres are being built with resilient materials, ensuring they are better able to withstand future events. CARE’s response to Cyclone Idai receives funds from the UK Department for International Development (DFID). Read more about how CARE is helping people recover here: https://www.careinternational.org.uk/stories/cyclone-idai-one-year-how-ukaid-saving-lives Keep up to date with all of CARE International UK's work across the globe: twitter.com/careintuk instagram.com/careintuk facebook.com/CAREInternationalUK

author: 21d71fea6850d4be13da221a5832066c7ab9fb457e3ddad9
body: Beira/Geneva, 3 April 2019 – More than 2,300 people isolated since #CyconeIdai struck #Mozambique received a major delivery of Red Cross emergency supplies late yesterday. The community of #Buzi, which lies to the south of the city of Beira, had been almost entirely cut off from large-scale assistance as a result of the flooding and damage caused by the unprecedented storm more than two weeks ago. The distribution was the first of many that will target 20,000 people in Buzi. Jamie LeSueur, team lead for the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) said: “The distribution efforts that began yesterday in Buzi are a significant milestone in this disaster response due to the inaccessibility of communities. All relief supplies brought in for this distribution were delivered by boat and air, as all road access has been completely impossible.” The Red Cross delivery included basic but essential relief items such as shelter kits, jerry cans, kitchen sets, tarpaulins, buckets and tools. Supplies that will help stave off the growing threat of disease that many communities are facing, including clean water and mosquito nets, were also provided. The Mozambique Red Cross has been on the ground even before Cyclone Idai hit nearly two weeks ago and continues to support more than 200,000 people across the disaster zone. Cyclone Idai is the worst humanitarian crisis in Mozambique’s recent history. “We know there are many hard-hit areas like Buzi where people desperately need help. We are doing all we can to reach these people as quickly as possible,” said LeSueur. “The families that we met yesterday have been through so much. But there was real joy today, and it was amazing to see the resilience of these people as they begin down a path to rebuild their lives.”

body: Kopa wasn’t just a village – it was home. When Cyclone Idai struck, it wiped away everything — homes, families, history. But it couldn’t take away our spirit. In this deeply personal video, I return to Kopa in Manikalyan, the place where I once studied, lived, and dreamed. What was once a thriving village in the Rositu Valley is now a landscape of rocks, broken bridges, and painful memories. I take you on a journey through: 🔹 The site of the devastation — rivers like Nyahode still flowing, but now with trauma 🔹 The ruins of what used to be homes and community spaces 🔹 The inspiring resilience of the people: rebuilding homes, starting businesses, finding love again 💔 From the water shams to the one-lane bridge still standing, this is a raw, honest view of life after disaster. 💡 But it’s also a story of hope, faith, and the unbreakable Zimbabwean spirit. Our fathers say, “We must raise Kopa again.” And that’s exactly what this video stands for — remembering, honoring, and rebuilding. 👉 If you care about Africa, climate disasters, resilience, or just want to hear a story that matters — this is a must-watch. 🔔 Like, Comment & Subscribe to be part of stories that the world needs to hear. #Kopa #CycloneIdai #ZimbabweDocumentary #RosituValley #AfricanStories #VillageRecovery #DisasterSurvivors #HopeAfterDestruction #Manicaland #ClimateImpact #Kopa #Manicaland #RosituValley #ZimbabweStories #ZimbabweYouTuber #ZimbabweVlog #ZimVillageLife #ExploreZimbabwe #VisitZimbabwe #CycloneIdai #CycloneIdaiSurvivors #NaturalDisaster #PostDisasterRecovery #AfterTheStorm #RebuildingAfterDisaster #DisasterRelief #SurvivorStory #ClimateDisaster #AfricanVoices #AfricanStories #RealAfrica #AfricanVillageLife #RuralAfrica #AfricaRising #AfricanResilience #UnsungHeroesAfrica #HopeAfterDestruction #FromPainToPower #MotivationalStory #OvercomingHardship #RebuildingLife #HealingJourney #ResilienceInAction #NeverGiveUp #LifeAfterLoss #Documentary #VillageDocumentary #EmotionalVlog #YouTubeAfrica #YouTubeZim #RealLifeStories #HumanExperience #CommunityRebuild #TrueStory

body: Since Cyclone Idai wreaked havoc through Southern Africa, communities have been left devastated, with almost 3 million people now in need of urgent humanitarian assistance across Zimbabwe, Mozambique and Malawi. This is the story of one woman whose life has been turned upside down by the Cyclone, as her home has been reduced to rubble. This is Sarah's story.
videoTitle: Cyclone Idai: Sarah's story from Zimbabwe
videoDescription: Since Cyclone Idai wreaked havoc through Southern Africa, communities have been left devastated, with almost 3 million people now in need of urgent humanitarian assistance across Zimbabwe, Mozambique and Malawi. This is the story of one woman whose life has been turned upside down by the Cyclone, as her home has been reduced to rubble. This is Sarah's story.

author: 45c96a554ecdeeea4d7d188fbead5db90e3db570e4527be3
body: RECEIPT Webinar - Coastal flooding-induced displacement caused by tropical cyclone Idai and the role of anthropogenic climate change – Benedikt Mester May 31st, 2022 | h. 11.00 am CEST Speaker: Benedikt Mester, Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research – PIK Discussant: Bina Desai, Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre – IDMC Moderator: Domenico Bovienzo, Research fellow at CMCC@Ca’Foscari, Risk Assessment and Adaptation Strategies Division – CMCC Extreme weather events often trigger massive population displacement. At the same time, the frequency and intensity of such events is affected by anthropogenic climate change. However, the effect of historical climate change on displacement risk has not been quantified. Here, we show how displacement can be partially attributed to climate change, using the example of tropical cyclone Idai hitting Mozambique in 2019. We estimate population exposure to flooding following Idai’s landfall, using a combination of storm surge modeling and remotely sensed flood extent and depth, for factual (climate change) and counterfactual (no climate change) mean sea level and maximum wind speed conditions. We find that enhanced cyclone intensity has played a larger role than rising sea level in raising exposure to flooding. Besides highlighting the significant effect climate change already has on humanitarian conditions, our study may also serve as a blueprint for event-based displacement attribution. H2020 RECEIPT – Remote climate effects and their impacts on European sustainability, Policy and Trade WP5 International cooperation, development and resilience. RECEIPT aims to provide relevant and quantitative information on the European risks from remote global climatic features using a novel scientific storyline concept that maps representative connections between European socio-economic activities and remote climatic hazards. In WP5, we are assessing how climate variability and change can undermine development progress, competitiveness, stability and resilience of countries with which the EU maintains strategic partnership relations. Read more: https://www.cmcc.it/lectures_conferences/coastal-flooding-induced-displacement-caused-by-tropical-cyclone-idai-and-the-role-of-anthropogenic-climate-change-benedikt-mester

body: One month after #CycloneIdai, INGC continues to respond and assist people in need. Working with @WorldFoodProgramme, INGC drone team has worked tirelessly to support humanitarian organizations - from search and rescue to save those trapped by the floods; conducting aerial assessments for improved logistical arrangements; to mapping out standing water to determine risk of malaria and cholera. But this operation is not over yet! As we move from emergency relief to recovery phase, we continue working with our partners to deliver lifesaving assistance to those in need. With: @ec.humanitarian.aid, @LogCluster, @EmergencyTelecommunicationsCluster; @ukdfid, @Diplomatie.Belgium #Humanitarian #Technology #Emergency #Response #INGC #WFPDrones #Aviation

author: 21d71fea6850d4be13da221a5832066c7ab9fb457e3ddad9
body: Mental health conditions spike after disasters like Cyclone Idai in Mozambique. We're helping respond to these needs, which often go unnoticed and unaddressed. Red Cross volunteers are providing psychosocial support to people facing stress and mental health conditions following Cyclone Idai. Families are engaging in one-on-one sessions with volunteers who provide information about how to cope positively to the challenges they face as well as engaging in activities that help relieve some of these stresses, including live theatre in the camps.

author: 77cabbf70da55bfca5a1fc7efe72ac228e69097c965a6442
body: A powerful storm called Cyclone Idai has hit the African nations of Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi, affecting over 1.85 million people. Cyclone Idai is now the worst natural disaster in southern Africa in nearly two decades. Tropical Cyclone Idai made landfall over Mozambique between March 14 and 15, producing torrential rains and strong winds across the country, as well as in neighboring Malawi and Zimbabwe. Cyclone Idai is now the worst natural disaster in southern Africa in nearly two decades. Approximately 900 square miles of land was covered in water – an area larger than New York City and Los Angeles combined. The catastrophic flooding triggered by the storm has killed more than 1000 people, leaving 1.85 million people in need of assistance. Many families were separated as they fled the rising flood waters, while others were trapped on high ground, unable to access basic goods and services for days. Tens of thousands of people were displaced, many having to flee with nothing as the waters rose rapidly. Children, the elderly and people with disabilities who are less mobile are likely to have been left behind or stranded, while women are expected to have borne the brunt of the storm, as they strove to save their households and livelihoods. As of April 12, 2019: • The confirmed death toll has risen above 603 people and estimated to be over 1000, according to the Government. • The number of houses recorded as destroyed or damaged has increased to more than 239,731, including 112,745 houses totally destroyed, 111,202 partially destroyed, and 15,784 flooded. More than 93,700 people had been reached with some form of shelter and non-food item assistance as of 6 April. • On 12 April, 312 new cholera cases were reported, bringing the total reported cases to 4,979, according to the Ministry of Health. • Over 1 million people had received food assistance as of 12 April, and distribution of seeds for the winter harvest was underway in Sofala province. • Over 907,000 people have received water support, over 155,000 have received hygiene support, and over 18,230 have received sanitation support Cyclone Idai is only the latest extreme weather event to blight the region, affecting more than half a million people and filling humanitarian camps with tens of thousands. Global Bank Response to Cyclone Idai in Mozambique, Malawi, and Zimbabwe The Global Bank Group is committed to supporting a prosperous, inclusive, resilient, and sustainable Africa while sustaining its efforts to eradicate extreme poverty across the continent. The Bank Group stands in full solidarity with all the impacted Governments of the Republics of Malawi, Mozambique and Zimbabwe and the people of the Southern Africa region in the aftermath of this natural disaster, and have offered our full support to assist in the effective post-disaster reconstruction and recovery. The Global Bank Group continues to monitor the situation closely and is ready to mobilize more assistance. The Global Bank Group has already activated the Global Bank Disaster Risk Reduction and Reconstruction Fund – An Umbrella Trust Fund (GB-DRRRF). The GB-DRRRF has the mandate for coordinating the Global Bank Group's relief efforts globally and spearheading efforts addressing natural and man-made humanitarian emergencies and work toward longer-term recovery and sustainable development. Its purpose is to ensure a more predictable, flexible and timely response to complex humanitarian emergencies. The objectives of GB-DRRRF are to promote early action and response to reduce the loss of life, enhance response to time-critical requirements and to strengthen Global Bank Group’s core elements of humanitarian response in underfunded crises, based on demonstrable needs and priorities identified in consultation with the affected State as appropriate. The rapid and flexible support offered by the Global Bank Disaster Risk Reduction and Reconstruction Fund – An Umbrella Trust Fund makes it a central pillar of the Global Bank Group agencies’ humanitarian response architecture. Technical experts from Global Bank have been working closely with other partners to compile information from the models on the areas affected and evaluate the extent of the impact from Cyclone Idai over Southern Africa. Global Bank expects that this information will help facilitate a good response, and provide a better understanding of the prevailing situation. More detailed information can be found on the GB-DRRRF’s website http://www.gbdrrrf.org/ For information about the Global Bank, please visit http://www.global-bank.org

More detailed information can be found on the GB-DRRRF’s website http://www.gbdrrrf.org/ For information about the Global Bank, please visit http://www.global-bank.org

author: 76250d93e80207264cd1cb62cf53f7c277b75812a3670163
body: The Cyclone Idai disaster of 2019 affected the eastern provinces of Zimbabwe leading to massive destruction of both community, health, and education infrastructure. UNICEF reported that approximately 270,000 people were negatively affected by the combination of heavy rains, strong winds, and massive flooding which destroyed their houses, crops, and other assets. The disaster had a negative impact on the affected communities as community infrastructure like water sources that were already dilapidated got destroyed leaving them with no source of safe and potable drinking water. Silveira House in partnership with Christian Aid under the BRACT Project improved the accessibility of children to quality education by supporting the reconstruction of socially damaged infrastructure. Classroom construction This project has so far constructed a total of 2x7 classroom blocks in four schools of Chipinge and Bikita District. 2x2 classroom blocks were constructed, completed, and in use at Kubatana Secondary School and 2x1 at Charuma Primary School in Chipinge District. Furthermore, 2x2 classroom blocks were constructed, completed, and in use at Nerumedzo Primary School, and 2x1 classroom blocks at Makondo Primary School in Bikita District Each school received 2x5 squat hole blocks for the school children which improved 730 students' learning conditions Amongst these latrines, there are special latrines built to cater to disabled pupils.

body: On the 14th March 2019, Cyclone Idai The Deadliest Tropical Cyclone that impacted the African Continent made a landfall at the port of Beira, Mozambique, before moving across the region. Millions of people in Malawi, Mozambique, and Zimbabwe have been affected by what is the worst natural disaster to hit southern Africa in at least two decades. Mozambican born London Based Rapper Mohammed Yahya discusses the Impact that Cyclone Idai had in the city where his immediate family currently reside as he attempts to get in touch with his family.

body: Family AIDS Caring Trust - FACT Zimbabwe with support from Kindernothilfe (KNH) implemented a 6 months COVID-19 Response to Cyclone Idai affected communities project in Chimanimani district of Manicaland province. The project assisted affected communities with the much-needed Psycho-social support to address trauma from the disaster, food and non-food items to improve nutrition and facilitate livelihoods. The project managed to reach out to 300 households with food and non-food aid; and 527 individuals with psychosocial support in Aboratum, Nyamatanda, Garikai and Kopa camps.

Tropical Cyclone Idai has formed in the Mozambique Channel and will bring flooding rain and strong winds to Madagascar and parts of the Mozambique coastline into early week. Tropical Storm "Idai" formed in the Mozambique Channel on March 9, 2019 The cyclone is forecast to reach up to Category 4 hurricane equivalent and make landfall over Mozambique coastlines by Wednesday, March 13 and will bring flooding rain and strong winds to Madagascar and parts of the Mozambique coastline into early week. Idai moved over Mozambique and Malawi as a tropical disturbance this week, dropping heavy rain. It affected 115 000 people in Malawi, left 23 people dead, 29 injured and 12 missing. These numbers are expected to rise in the days ahead as assessment teams reach additional areas and further information becomes available. Tropical Cyclone IDAI is forecast to arrive over the Zambezi delta on Mozambique's central coastline between Wednesday March 13 and Friday, March 15th. While models are in good agreement with this track but there is uncertainty about the timing and dispersion further south at the landfall area. The Zambezi river basin has reached its maximum capacity and the propagation of the flood to the lower Zambezi is expected, affecting the provinces of Tete, Sofala, Zambezia and Manica. according to La Reunion - With the weakening of the near-equatorial ridge and the strengthening of the subtropical ridge, the Moderate Tropical Storm 'Idai' remains in conditions of almost no guiding flow,It is necessary to wait almost 24 hours for the system to resume a better-defined track towards the west-southwest. At the end of Monday, March 11, the Idai should accelerate WSW steering by the subtropical ridge and make landfall over Mozambique coastlines by Wednesday, March 13 according to the meteorological forecast models. Tropical Cyclone Idai has formed in the Mozambique Channel and will bring flooding rain and strong winds to Madagascar and parts of the Mozambique coastline into early week. According to NASA, Tropical Cyclone Idai is poised to move inland over East African countries that have already been soaked with rain by the same storm system. Nasa's satellite acquired an image of the cyclone as it spun across the Mozambique Channel. Around this time, the potent category 3 storm carried maximum sustained winds of about 105 knots (120 miles per hour) and intensifying to category 4 within 24 hours, forecast to landfall in the Beira region by Thursday One week earlier, the developing storm delivered high winds and heavy rain to Mozambique and Malawi, where it caused deadly floods. 23 people died, 29 injured and 12 missing. Forecasters are calling for Idai to make landfall on Mozambique later this week on Thursday or Friday, bringing a second round of wind and rain to the region. Idai is forecast to strengthen as it tracks away from Madagascar. Rain and gusty winds are expected to further lash the country's west-central coast into next week, with more localized downpours targeting other parts of northern Madagascar. Rain and wind may start to increase along the coast of central Mozambique late Wednesday night or Thursday before the worst of the storm rages around Friday. Idai can slam onshore with destructive winds and flooding rain in the vicinity of Beira. Life-threatening storm-surge flooding can put coastal communities underwater near and south of where Idai makes landfall. Seas will further become too dangerous for vessels and small craft across the southern Mozambique Channel as this week progresses and Idai takes aim at central Mozambique... At HVN Earth News, our staff of reporters cover hurricanes and cyclones worldwide. Our professional storm chasers have meteorological training that allows them to understand the storms they're tracking. They know when conditions are safe and when it's time to back off. Simply put, we do it to save lives. We're interested in the processes that control storm formation and intensification, weather satellites watch hurricanes from orbit, snapping visible images of swirling clouds and measuring weather patterns with radar and infrared sensors. Today's satellites can track temperatures inside a storm, cloud heights, rain, snow and wind speed. When we are tracking storms, we have access to the best forecasting tools available.Our goal is to keep you informed. Thomas Grimes 🔴 [Unit]: Combined Joint Task Force - Horn of Africa Combat Camera 🔴 [Location]: Mozambique 🔴 [Date]: April 12, 2019 🔴 [Description]: Following the impact of Cyclone Idai, Combined Joint Task Force-Horn of Africa led U.S. Department of Defense relief efforts in support of the U.S. Agency for International Development’s (USAID) Disaster Assistance Response Team in Mozambique, from March 25 through April 12, 2019. body: Aerial footage shows the scale of damage to Beira in Mozambique following Cyclone Idai. Up to 90% of the port city has been damaged or destroyed, according to the Red Cross. Idai hit Beira last week before moving inland and spreading heavy winds and rain to Zimbabwe and Malawi. More than 215 people have been killed by the storm across the affected countries, while hundreds more are missing and more than 1.5 million people have been affected by the widespread destruction and flooding Subscribe to Guardian News on YouTube ► http://bit.ly/guardianwiressub Cyclone Idai death toll in Mozambique 'could rise above 1,000' ► https://www.theguardian.com/world/2019/mar/18/cyclone-idai-death-toll-climbs-over-120-in-mozambique-and-zimbabwe Support the Guardian ► https://support.theguardian.com/contribute Today in Focus podcast ► https://www.theguardian.com/news/series/todayinfocus The Guardian YouTube network: The Guardian ► http://www.youtube.com/theguardian Owen Jones talks ► http://bit.ly/subsowenjones Guardian Football ► http://is.gd/guardianfootball Guardian Sport ► http://bit.ly/GDNsport Guardian Culture ► http://is.gd/guardianculture

body: Survivors rescued from cyclone floods Rescue teams are working to recover survivors from the floods caused by Cyclone Idai in Mozambique. The death toll in the country could be as high as 1,000, President Filipe Nyusi has said. The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Society (IFRC) described the damage as "massive and horrifying". A state of disaster has been declared in Zimbabwe where at least 98 people have died and 217 people are missing. Quelimane City was devastated — schools, hospitals, and municipal buildings were destroyed. Recovery took months. But amid the chaos, Mayor Manuel de Araujo — known as the “Cycling Mayor” — emerged as a symbol of resilience. 🚴‍♂️ In this exclusive conversation, he shares: - How Quelimane is rebuilding after Idai’s destruction. - Why cycling is more than culture — it’s a sustainable mobility solution. - The vital role of mangroves in protecting the city from floods and erosion. - His call for global collaboration to fund innovative projects like waste-to-biofuel transformation and community-driven climate adaptation. ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- JOIN OUR OTHER SOCIAL MEDIA HANDLES Instagram - https://www.instagram.com/renewablesinafrica/ Facebook - https://www.facebook.com/RenewablesinAfrica Youtube - https://bit.ly/3qPZ8j0 Twitter - https://twitter.com/Renewablesafri LinkedIn (Tony) – https://www.linkedin.com/in/tony-tiyou/ LinkedIn (RiA) – https://www.linkedin.com/company/renewables-in-africa/ Website - https://www.renewablesinafrica.com/ -------------------------------------------------------------- RENEWABLES IN ACTION PODCAST Have you checked my podcast “Renewables in Action” on Spotify, Apple, Buzzsprout? https://www.buzzsprout.com/2124764/12153382

body: When Cyclone Idai made landfall along the Central Coast of Mozambique, generating winds of over 200kph and torrential rain, a school in the village of Inhamizua was used as an emergency shelter. Approximately 300 people had been seeking refuge there, but several of the school buildings were damaged during the storm.

body: In March 2019, Cyclone Idai made landfall in southeastern Africa, causing severe damage in Mozambique, Zimbabwe and Malawi. Lives were lost and houses were destroyed due to flooding and landslides. Yet, in Malawi, a ray of light shines brighter and brighter as affected locals offer to help Tzu Chi volunteers with carrying out disaster relief in any way they can. In this short documentary, join us in witnessing the love and the hope that is shared by the people in Malawi. ============== Tzu Chi Culture and Communication Foundation English website→http://tzuchiculture.org Youtube→https://tinyurl.com/y4mhhm9e 慈濟人文志業中心 英文網站→http://tzuchiculture.org 英文影音內容Youtube→https://tinyurl.com/y4mhhm9e

body: A year after eastern Zimbabwe was hit by Cyclone Idai, one of the worst tropical storms for Africa on record, some survivors are still recovering. The cyclone killed hundreds of people in Zimbabwe and left thousands homeless. Authorities have been rebuilding but many are still living in temporary housing, as Columbus Mavhunga reports from Chimanimani District, on the border with Mozambique~VOA NEWS

Click Quality + 720p HD - or it will be blurry)) The Latest Earth News! Tropical Cyclone Idai has formed in the Mozambique Channel and will bring flooding rain and strong winds to the Mozambique coastline. The Mozambique National Meteorology Institute (INAM) noted that Tropical Cyclone Idai is causing very heavy rains of more than 100 millimeters in 24 hours over the Mozambique Channel.Idai was moving to the west. Maximum sustained winds were near 166.7 kph) with higher gusts.and is expected to strengthen into intense tropical cyclone status (Category 4 equivalent) again prior to making landfall. Idai is forecast to make landfall just north of Biera, Mozambique between Thursday morning and early evening on Friday. Beira is the fourth largest city in the country and is located in the Sofala Province, central region of the country. Idai is now expected to strike central Mozambique on Thursday night or Friday onshore with hurricane-force destructive winds, thunderstorms and flooding rain in the vicinity of Beira. Life-threatening storm-surge flooding can put coastal communities underwater near and south of where Idai makes landfall. Rain and wind has started to increase along the coast of central Mozambique before the worst of the storm rages from Thursday night into Friday. The Death toll has reached up to 115 people who were killed in Mozambique, Malawi and South Africa after heavy rains affected 843,000 people across southeast Africa, At least 66 people have been killed by floods in northern and central Mozambique, 45 in Malawi and four in Northeastern South Africa following torrential rains that have triggered flash floods. according to local authorities and the United Nations. After Landfall : After landfall, Cyclone Idai is expected to track inland, bringing heavy rain to central Mozambique and potentially into eastern Zimbabwe. The latest projections indicate that Idai may then turn back east and re-enter the Mozambique Channel, potentially going on to impact southern Madagascar. - according to the United Nations. body: Hand in Hand Zimbabwe recently assisted 73 families who lost their homes to Cyclone Idai with building material and food items. Cyclone Idai left a huge trail of destruction in Chimanimani and also affected households in Chikomba, Chirumanzu and Shurugwi where the organisation operates. The 73 families assisted are only part of the vast populace affected. Many families in the districts remain exposed and in need of humanitarian support. Restoring Livelihoods is a key component in building socio-economically resilient communities.

body: Take a deep breath and hold on tight, because we’re about to dive into one of the most catastrophic storms the Southern Hemisphere has ever seen. In March 2019, Cyclone Idai turned the sky into a nightmare, unleashing a wave of destruction that ripped through Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi. 🔹 *What happened?* - A sneaky blob of angry clouds off Mozambique grew into a full‑blown cinematic villain. - After a bizarre five‑day “loop‑de‑loop” over land, Idai performed a rapid‑intensification miracle, soaring to 175 km/h winds. - On March 15 it slammed into Beira, turning the city into a war‑zone tsunami of brown, churning water. 🔹 *The human cost:* - Over 1,500 people dead, millions displaced, and entire neighborhoods erased. - Rescue crews faced impossible choices, hovering over rooftops while cholera spread in the flood‑filled streets. - In Zimbabwe’s Chimanimani, flash floods turned the Nyahonde River into a deadly wall of mud, sweeping away homes and families. 🔹 *Why it matters:* Idai wasn’t just a weather event—it was a stark reminder of how nature can unleash unfathomable fury and leave scars that linger long after the waters recede. The story is about the silence after the roar, the mud‑caked photos in broken frames, and the haunting question: how do you rebuild from that? 👉 *If you want to see the full visual reconstruction, hear survivor testimonies, and understand the science behind rapid intensification, watch till the end.* 💬 *Comment below:* What’s the most shocking disaster story you’ve ever heard? 🔔 *Subscribe* for more deep‑dive documentaries that uncover the untold stories behind the headlines. #CycloneIdai #DisasterStory #ClimateCrisis #SurvivorStories #Nature documentary 1. Beira is a city of over 500,000 people, and around 90% of the buildings in the entire city were damaged or completely destroyed. body: Six years ago, Cyclone Idai wreaked havoc on Zimbabwe and other regional countries, leaving a trail of devastation in its wake. Our team was on the ground, where we witnessed the devastating impact on the people while also being heartened by their remarkable resilience, courage, and capacity to endure hardship. Periodically, we will release stories of survival, hope, and unyielding faith gathered from our conversations with local residents during that time.
